title: Macramé mug cosy
subtitle: A knotted sleeve for a 350 ml mug, about 22 cm circumference x 9 cm tall
excerpt: A knotted sleeve that wraps a 350 ml mug, roughly 22 cm in circumference and 9 cm tall, worked in alternating square knots on 3 mm cotton macramé cord. The sleeve mounts on a starter cord, buttons at the back with a single overhand-knot button through a cord loop, and is sized by a swatch test before cutting full cord lengths.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The alternating-square-knot body and overhand-knot button are standard macramé constructions from the mid-20th-century revival. Knot structures referenced in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work (1939), Cornell Maritime Press. The swatch-and-measure approach for fitting a knotted piece to a cylindrical form is standard practice in the modern macramé guild tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-loop mounting knot used to attach a folded cord to a ring, dowel, or holding cord. The cord is folded in half, the loop is passed over the mount from front to back, and both tails are pulled through the loop and tightened. The standard starting knot for macramé panels.

A grid pattern made by tying square knots in groups of four cords, then offsetting the next row so each new knot straddles cords from two adjacent groups. Alternating the grouping row by row produces a diagonal lattice and distributes tension evenly across the full piece width.

The simplest closed knot: the working end is looped over the standing cord and back through the loop, forming a single symmetrical knot. A tied overhand knot on a short cord stub acts as a button that will not pass back through a loop of slightly smaller diameter.

A short knotted sample worked in the same cord and knot pattern as the main piece, used to measure the knot density before cutting full cord lengths. The swatch row count per centimetre gives the number of rows needed for the finished height. Because knot density varies with cord diameter and tightness, the swatch test is the only reliable way to size a piece to a specific form.

content.text: This knotted sleeve wraps a standard 350 ml mug, the size used by most domestic mugs and most coffee-shop-style cups. Finished dimensions are roughly 22 cm in circumference and 9 cm tall. The sleeve mounts on a short starter cord, is worked flat in
alternating square knots
and then joined at the back with a button-and-loop closure. The button is a single
overhand knot
tied on a short cord stub; the loop is formed by leaving a gap in the body selvedge. Before cutting cord for the full sleeve, run a
swatch test
to measure your knot density. Use 3 mm cotton macramé cord throughout; twisted 3-ply gives a firm but slightly padded surface that insulates the hand from the mug wall.
What you need

items.name: 3 mm cotton macramé cord, natural or dyed
items.qty: About 12 m for the sleeve and button
items.substitution: 3 mm single-strand cotton can be used; it produces a slightly denser knot grid and a flatter surface

items.name: The mug you are sizing the sleeve to

content.text: The knots, step by step
The sleeve uses three knot types. The
lark's head
mounts all working cords onto the starter cord. The alternating square knot builds the body. The overhand knot forms the button closure.
Lark's head: fold a working cord in half, pass the loop over the starter cord from front to back, bring both tails through the loop, and pull firmly. Repeat across all cords.

definition.caption: Lark's head: fold cord over the starter cord, pull both tails through the loop and tighten.

content.text: Square knot: using four cords (two outer working cords around two inner filler cords), take the left working cord over both filler cords and under the right working cord. Take the right working cord under both filler cords and up through the loop. Pull both working cords outward firmly. Reverse the move to complete the square: left under fillers, right over fillers and through the new loop. Pull evenly.

definition.caption: Square knot: two working cords around two filler cords, completed in two moves.

content.text: Overhand knot button: cut a short stub of cord at 6 cm. Fold it in half to find the midpoint, then tie a single overhand knot in both strands together close to the midpoint. Pull firmly so the knot is compact. The two short tails are sewn or tucked into the back of the sleeve to fix the button in place. The knot itself passes through a cord loop left in the selvedge.

definition.caption: Overhand knot: a single loop-and-through knot that forms the button on the sleeve closure.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Run the swatch test

The swatch test measures how many alternating-square-knot rows your tension produces per centimetre. Cut four cords at 60 cm each and two filler cords at 20 cm each, mount the two filler cords side by side on a short holder cord pinned to the board, and mount all four working cords with lark's head knots. Tie 10 rows of alternating square knots, keeping the same tension you plan to use throughout the sleeve. Measure the 10-row height. Divide 10 by the measured height in centimetres to get your rows per cm.

For example: if 10 rows measure 5 cm, your knot density is 2 rows per cm. For a 9 cm tall sleeve you need 18 rows. Also measure the swatch width to confirm your stitch gauge: for a 22 cm circumference with the button and loop taking about 2 cm of the circumference, the knotted section should be about 20 cm wide. Adjust the number of cord groups if your swatch width does not match.
Run the swatch test before cutting any cord for the sleeve. Cutting full lengths before testing risks running short if your tension is tighter than expected.
Step 2: Calculate and cut the working cords

For a 20 cm wide sleeve in alternating square knots, you need a cord group of four working cords for every 2 to 2.5 cm of finished width, giving roughly 8 to 10 groups of four, or 32 to 40 working cords total (16 to 20 folded cords mounted with lark's head knots). The exact count depends on your swatch width measurement.

Working cord length formula: cut each cord at four times the desired finished height plus 20 cm for mounting and finishing. For a 9 cm finished height: 4 x 9 cm = 36 cm, plus 20 cm = 56 cm per cord, rounded up to 60 cm. If your swatch shows your tension is tighter than average (rows less than 0.4 cm tall), cut cords at 65 cm to allow for the extra cord consumption per knot.

Also cut one starter cord at 30 cm for the mount. This starter cord is removed or hidden after the first row is set; it holds the piece steady while the first row is tied and can be pulled free from the lark's head mounts once the row is firm.
Step 3: Mount and tie the first row

Pin the starter cord horizontally to the macramé board, stretched taut. Mount all working cords with lark's head knots, packing them closely side by side across the 20 cm width. After mounting, you have a row of working ends hanging below the starter cord.

Tie the first row of square knots across the full width: working left to right, group the cords into sets of four (cords 1-4, cords 5-8, and so on across the full width) and tie one square knot in each group. Leave 3 to 4 mm of space above each knot; this spacing is what produces the open lattice look rather than a dense, close-knotted surface. Keep all knots at the same height from the starter cord by pinning the work flat and measuring from the cord to each knot before tying.
Step 4: Build the alternating square knot body

Tie the second row with offset groupings: skip the first two cords at the left edge, group the next four cords (cords 3, 4, 5, 6) and tie a square knot. Continue across the width with the same offset grouping. Skip the last two cords at the right edge. The row 2 knots sit between the row 1 knots, which is the alternating pattern.

Continue alternating rows: row 3 matches row 1, row 4 matches row 2, and so on. After every four rows, hold the sleeve up to the mug and check that the piece is tracking straight and at the correct width. Pin both outer edges to the board after each row to prevent the piece from drawing in on the sides.
When the knotted body reaches one row short of the measured row count for your 9 cm height, stop and prepare the button loop before tying the final row.
Step 5: Form the button loop

The loop is formed at one selvedge edge of the sleeve. On the right edge, identify the outermost four cords. Instead of tying the final row's outermost knot, tie an overhand knot in the two outermost cords together, leaving a 15 mm cord loop between this overhand knot and the last knot in the row above. This gap becomes the button loop. The overhand knot holds the loop open and prevents the edge from unravelling.

Tie the final row across the remaining cords, skipping the loop-forming cords. Check the loop size against your overhand-knot button stub: the loop should pass over the button knot with a small amount of resistance, not loosely. If the loop is too large, tie the overhand-knot on the loop itself to take up the excess. If it is too small, cut a new section of the selvedge cord and start the loop again.
Step 6: Finish the top and bottom edges

At the bottom edge (where the working cord ends hang), trim all ends to 3 cm and tie one overhand knot in each cord end to prevent fraying, or apply a small dab of clear fabric glue to each cut end. The overhand-knot finish gives a clean row of small knot bumps along the lower edge; the glued finish is invisible.

At the top edge, remove or slide out the starter cord. The lark's head mounts sit as a finished top edge; trim or tuck any loose cord tail from the starter cord. If the starter cord left a visible gap at the very top, tie an overhand knot in each pair of lark's head cords to close the edge.
Step 7: Make and attach the button

Cut a 6 cm stub of cord. Hold both ends together and tie a single overhand knot in both strands at the midpoint. Pull the knot compact and firm. The two short tails from the button extend about 2 cm on each side. Position the button on the opposite selvedge edge from the loop, at the mid-height of the sleeve, and thread both tails through the knotted body from front to back using the end of the scissors or a blunt needle. Tie the two tails together at the back with one overhand knot pulled tight against the body. Trim the tails to 1 cm. The button sits proud on the front of the sleeve, centred on the opposing edge from the loop.
Step 8: Fit and adjust

Wrap the sleeve around the mug and pass the button loop over the button knot. The sleeve should sit snugly against the mug wall without puckering or pulling. If the sleeve is too wide, the outer selvedge cords can be woven back through the edge row to draw in 5 to 10 mm on each side. If the sleeve is too narrow, a row of slip-half-hitches at the selvedge can ease it out slightly. Most fit issues from a well-run swatch test should be small enough to resolve at the finishing stage.
Variations

A double half-hitch border at the top and bottom edges gives a more defined frame. Lay one cord horizontally across all working cords above the first knot row and work each working cord around it with a double half-hitch, left to right, producing a firm horizontal bar. Repeat the same bar below the last row using a separate holding cord. The two horizontal bars frame the alternating-square lattice.

A solid gathered section at the base instead of individual fringe ends uses a gathering knot to collect all the lower cords into a single bound finish, then trims to a short fringe. This works well on a mug cosy because the gathered finish is neat against the table surface.

For a tall mug (400 ml or a standard travel mug at about 12 cm tall), add extra rows until the sleeve height matches the mug. Run the swatch test again with the taller cord length to confirm the cord consumption is still within the cut-cord formula.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The sleeve slides down the mug or does not sit snugly
items.cause: The finished sleeve is too wide. This usually means the swatch width was not measured before cutting cord, or the knot spacing in the sleeve was looser than in the swatch
items.fix: Weave the selvedge cords from the outer edge back into the last row to draw in each side by 5 to 10 mm. A small amount of drawing-in at both edges brings the sleeve to the correct circumference without re-knotting the body. For future sleeves, pin the swatch to the board and measure its width carefully before scaling up the cord count.

items.problem: The alternating-square lattice has an uneven texture, with some rows tighter than others
items.cause: Tension varied between rows, or the knot spacing was not consistent across the piece
items.fix: Pin the work flat to the board after each row and measure the row height at both selvedge edges before tying the next row. Rows at the same height are evenly tensioned. If a row is tighter than the others, ease the knots slightly upward before the cord sets.

items.problem: The button loop is too small to pass over the button knot
items.cause: The overhand knot that formed the loop was tied too close to the previous row, leaving insufficient loop length
items.fix: Cut the overhand loop knot and re-tie it further from the previous row, allowing a 15 to 20 mm loop length. If the selvedge cord is now too short, use a new short length of cord knotted into the selvedge at that point.

items.problem: The working cords run short before the sleeve reaches the full row count
items.cause: The cord-cut formula was based on a lighter tension than used in the piece, or the swatch density was measured incorrectly
items.fix: There is no reliable way to extend short cords invisibly in a knotted piece. Plan to end one or two rows short and add a horizontal double-half-hitch border at the lower edge to fill the height, which uses its own separate cord lengths rather than the working cords.
